name: SA Culture & History
description: South African history, culture, traditions, languages and landmarks
author: Game Night Crew

questions.question: How many official languages does South Africa have?
questions.explanation: South Africa has 11 official languages — the most of any country in Africa! From Zulu to Afrikaans, we've got them all.

questions.question: What is the capital city where the South African Parliament sits?
questions.explanation: Parliament sits in Cape Town, our legislative capital. SA is one of few countries with three capitals — Pretoria (executive), Cape Town (legislative), and Bloemfontein (judicial).

questions.question: What colour is NOT on the South African flag?
questions.explanation: No purple on our flag! It's black, gold, green, white, red, and blue. Six colours making it one of the most colourful flags in the world.

questions.question: Nelson Mandela spent 27 years in prison. On which island was he held for the longest period?
questions.explanation: Robben Island, just off the coast of Cape Town. Madiba spent 18 of his 27 prison years there. It's now a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

questions.question: What is 'braai' in South African culture?
questions.explanation: A braai is our beloved barbecue! It's practically a national sport. Heritage Day on 24 September is even called National Braai Day by many South Africans.

questions.question: Which South African province is the smallest by area?
questions.explanation: Gauteng is the smallest province at about 18,178 km², but it's the economic powerhouse and most densely populated province in SA!

questions.question: What does the Zulu word 'ubuntu' roughly translate to?
questions.explanation: 'I am because we are' — ubuntu is the philosophy that a person exists through their relationships with others. It's at the heart of African culture.

questions.question: In what year did South Africa hold its first democratic election?
questions.explanation: 1994 — the year everything changed! On 27 April 1994, all South Africans could vote for the first time. We now celebrate it as Freedom Day.

questions.question: Which famous South African won the Nobel Peace Prize together with F.W. de Klerk in 1993?
questions.explanation: Nelson Mandela and F.W. de Klerk shared the 1993 Nobel Peace Prize for their work in peacefully ending apartheid. Desmond Tutu won his own Nobel Peace Prize back in 1984.

questions.question: The Springboks are South Africa's national team in which sport?
questions.explanation: The Springboks are our rugby boys! Winners of four Rugby World Cups (1995, 2007, 2019, 2023). Bokke!

questions.question: Which South African city is known as 'The City of Gold'?
questions.explanation: Johannesburg — eGoli in Zulu, literally meaning 'Place of Gold'. It was built on the Witwatersrand gold rush of 1886.

questions.question: How many provinces does South Africa have?
questions.explanation: Nine provinces! Gauteng, Western Cape, Eastern Cape, KwaZulu-Natal, Free State, Limpopo, Mpumalanga, North West, and Northern Cape.

questions.question: Which pass connects the Western Cape's inland Karoo region to the coast, and is one of SA's most famous mountain passes?
questions.explanation: Du Toitskloof Pass connects Paarl to Worcester over the Hawqua Mountains. While all of these are famous SA passes, Du Toitskloof is the classic Karoo-to-coast route through the Boland mountains.

questions.question: What is the traditional Afrikaans word for a farmer?
questions.explanation: 'Boer' literally means farmer in Afrikaans. The Boers were the Dutch-descended settlers who farmed the interior. Hence the Anglo-Boer Wars!

questions.question: In what year was the Sharpeville massacre, a turning point in anti-apartheid resistance?
questions.explanation: 21 March 1960 — police opened fire on a peaceful protest against pass laws in Sharpeville, killing 69 people. We now commemorate it as Human Rights Day.

questions.question: The San people are known for their rock art found across Southern Africa. What were they historically (and incorrectly) called by colonists?
questions.explanation: The San were called 'Bushmen' by European colonists — a term now considered outdated and disrespectful. Their rock art dates back over 20,000 years!

questions.question: Which of these languages uses click consonants?
questions.explanation: Xhosa (isiXhosa) is famous for its click consonants, inherited from the Khoisan languages. The 'X' in Xhosa is itself a click sound! Nelson Mandela was a Xhosa speaker.

questions.question: How many times have the Springboks won the Rugby World Cup (up to and including 2023)?
questions.explanation: Four times! 1995 (that iconic Madiba moment), 2007, 2019, and 2023. The most successful team in Rugby World Cup history. Bokke!

questions.question: Put these South African historical events in chronological order (earliest to latest): 1) First democratic election 2) Discovery of gold on the Witwatersrand 3) End of the Anglo-Boer War 4) Union of South Africa formed
questions.explanation: Gold discovered 1886, Anglo-Boer War ended 1902, Union formed 1910, first democratic election 1994. Quite a journey for Mzansi!

questions.question: District Six in Cape Town was forcibly cleared during apartheid. Approximately how many people were removed?
questions.explanation: About 60,000 people were forcibly removed from District Six from 1968 onwards under the Group Areas Act. Their homes were bulldozed. The District Six Museum keeps this painful history alive.

questions.question: What is the Cradle of Humankind, located near Johannesburg?
options: A UNESCO site with ancient hominid fossils
questions.explanation: It's a UNESCO World Heritage Site containing some of the oldest hominid fossils ever found, including 'Mrs Ples' and 'Little Foot'. We literally come from here!

questions.question: In what year was apartheid officially made law in South Africa?
questions.explanation: 1948 — when the National Party came to power and began formalising racial segregation into law. Though racial discrimination existed before, 1948 marked the start of official apartheid policy.

questions.question: Which famous South African author wrote 'Cry, the Beloved Country'?
questions.explanation: Alan Paton published 'Cry, the Beloved Country' in 1948. It tells the story of a Zulu pastor's journey to Johannesburg and became one of the most important South African novels ever written.

questions.question: The Great Trek refers to what event in South African history?
options: The migration of Zulu clans under Shaka
Boer settlers moving inland from the Cape Colony
The forced removal of people during apartheid
questions.explanation: The Great Trek (1836-1854) was when Boer settlers left the Cape Colony and moved into the interior to escape British rule. They became known as Voortrekkers.

questions.question: Shaka Zulu united the Zulu nation in the early 1800s. In roughly what year was he assassinated by his half-brothers?
questions.explanation: Shaka was assassinated in 1828 by his half-brothers Dingane and Mhlangana. Despite his short reign, he transformed the Zulu into one of the most powerful nations in southern Africa.

questions.question: Which South African won the Nobel Prize for Literature in 1991?
questions.explanation: Nadine Gordimer won in 1991 for her novels exploring the moral and racial complexities of apartheid. J.M. Coetzee won his Nobel in 2003 — SA has two literature laureates!

questions.question: The Mapungubwe Kingdom, which predates Great Zimbabwe, was located in which present-day South African province?
questions.explanation: Mapungubwe was in Limpopo, at the confluence of the Limpopo and Shashe rivers. It was a thriving kingdom from around 1075 to 1220 AD, and its famous golden rhino is a national treasure.

questions.question: How many Nobel Peace Prize winners has South Africa produced? (Count individuals, not shared prizes)
questions.explanation: Four! Albert Luthuli (1960), Desmond Tutu (1984), Nelson Mandela (1993), and F.W. de Klerk (1993). That's more than most countries can claim.

questions.question: Rank these South African cities from NORTH to SOUTH: 1) Cape Town 2) Durban 3) Polokwane 4) Johannesburg
questions.explanation: From north to south: Polokwane (Limpopo), Johannesburg (Gauteng), Durban (KZN coast), and Cape Town way down south. Polokwane is the most northerly major city in SA.

questions.question: What was the original name of the city now called Pretoria/Tshwane?
questions.explanation: Pretoria was founded and named Pretoria in 1855 after Voortrekker leader Andries Pretorius. The municipality was later renamed Tshwane, but the city itself has always been Pretoria.

questions.question: The Khoisan languages are famous for click consonants. How many distinct click types are recognized in the Khoisan language family?
questions.explanation: There are 5 basic click types in Khoisan languages: dental, lateral, palatal, alveolar, and bilabial. Some languages combine these with other sounds to create dozens of click consonants!

questions.question: In what year was the African National Congress (ANC) founded?
questions.explanation: The ANC was founded on 8 January 1912 in Bloemfontein as the South African Native National Congress. It's one of the oldest liberation movements in Africa.

questions.question: Which South African city hosted the famous Rivonia Trial where Mandela and others were sentenced?
questions.explanation: The Rivonia Trial took place at the Palace of Justice in Pretoria from 1963-1964. Though the arrests happened in Rivonia (near Joburg), the trial was held in Pretoria.

questions.question: What is the significance of the 'Nkosi Sikelel' iAfrika' portion of the national anthem?
options: It was composed by a British missionary
It was the anthem of the apartheid government
It was a hymn composed by Enoch Sontonga in 1897
It was written by Nelson Mandela in prison
questions.explanation: Enoch Sontonga, a Methodist school teacher, composed 'Nkosi Sikelel' iAfrika' (God Bless Africa) in 1897. It became a pan-African liberation hymn before being incorporated into SA's national anthem in 1994.

questions.question: The Battle of Isandlwana in 1879 was a major Zulu victory over the British. Roughly how many British soldiers were killed?
questions.explanation: About 1,300 British and allied troops were killed at Isandlwana on 22 January 1879. It was one of the worst defeats the British army ever suffered against an indigenous force.

questions.question: The Voortrekker Monument in Pretoria has a special design feature: once a year, sunlight shines through an opening onto a cenotaph. On which date does this happen?
questions.explanation: On 16 December at noon, a ray of sunlight falls on the cenotaph inscribed with 'Ons vir jou, Suid-Afrika'. The date commemorates the Battle of Blood River (1838).

questions.question: How many languages is the South African national anthem sung in?
questions.explanation: Five! The anthem is sung in Xhosa, Zulu, Sesotho, Afrikaans, and English. It's the only national anthem in the world that uses five languages. Truly a rainbow nation anthem!

questions.question: Which architect designed the Union Buildings in Pretoria?
questions.explanation: Sir Herbert Baker designed the Union Buildings, completed in 1913. He also designed other notable SA buildings and went on to help design government buildings in New Delhi, India.

questions.question: What was the name of the ship that brought the first Dutch settlers to the Cape in 1652?
questions.explanation: The Drommedaris was Jan van Riebeeck's flagship. He arrived with three ships — the Drommedaris, Reijger, and Goede Hoop — on 6 April 1652.

questions.question: Put these South African leaders in order of when they became president (earliest to latest): 1) Thabo Mbeki 2) Jacob Zuma 3) Nelson Mandela 4) Cyril Ramaphosa
questions.explanation: Mandela (1994), Mbeki (1999), Zuma (2009), Ramaphosa (2018). Each president shaped the post-apartheid era in their own way.

questions.question: The Cullinan Diamond, found near Pretoria in 1905, weighed how many carats in its uncut form?
questions.explanation: The Cullinan Diamond weighed a massive 3,106 carats — it's the largest gem-quality rough diamond ever found! It was cut into several stones, the largest of which sits in the British Crown Jewels.

questions.question: Which South African king led the Zulu forces at the Battle of Isandlwana?
questions.explanation: King Cetshwayo kaMpande was the Zulu king during the Anglo-Zulu War of 1879. His forces delivered a devastating defeat to the British at Isandlwana before ultimately losing the war.

questions.question: What was the original Khoi name for Table Mountain before European colonisation?
questions.explanation: The Khoi people called it Hoerikwaggo, meaning 'Mountain in the Sea'. The name was in use long before the Dutch arrived and renamed it Tafelberg.

questions.question: In what year was the Group Areas Act — one of the pillars of apartheid — first passed?
questions.explanation: The Group Areas Act was passed in 1950, just two years after the National Party came to power. It assigned racial groups to different residential and business areas across SA.

questions.question: The famous fossil 'Mrs Ples' found at Sterkfontein belongs to which hominid species?
questions.explanation: 'Mrs Ples' is an Australopithecus africanus skull estimated to be about 2.1 million years old. She was discovered by Robert Broom in 1947 at the Sterkfontein Caves.

questions.question: The Castle of Good Hope in Cape Town was completed in what year?
questions.explanation: The Castle of Good Hope was completed in 1679. It's the oldest surviving colonial building in South Africa and has been in continuous use since then — originally as a VOC fort.

questions.question: Which South African won the first Nobel Prize ever awarded to an African?
questions.explanation: Max Theiler, born in Pretoria, won the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1951 for developing a vaccine against yellow fever. Albert Luthuli was the first African to win the Peace Prize, in 1960.

questions.question: The Bambatha Rebellion of 1906 was a response to what?
options: The imposition of a poll tax on Zulu men
The annexation of Zululand by Britain
The removal of Zulu cattle by settlers
questions.explanation: Chief Bambatha kaMancinza led a rebellion against the British-imposed poll tax of £1 on all adult males in Natal. The rebellion was brutally suppressed, with thousands of Zulu killed.

questions.question: How many concentration camps did the British establish during the Anglo-Boer War (approximately)?
questions.explanation: The British set up approximately 45 concentration camps for Boer civilians and about 64 for Black Africans during the Anglo-Boer War (1899-1902). Around 28,000 Boer women and children died in these camps.

questions.question: Which ancient trade port on the KwaZulu-Natal coast was known to Arab and Swahili traders as early as the 10th century?
questions.explanation: Sodwana Bay and the surrounding coast were part of ancient trade routes. Arab traders sailed down the East African coast trading gold, ivory and other goods with local communities long before European arrival.

questions.question: In what year did Jan van Riebeeck plant the famous bitter almond hedge in Cape Town, meant to separate the settlement from the Khoikhoi?
questions.explanation: Van Riebeeck planted the hedge in 1660 — just 8 years after arriving at the Cape. Parts of this hedge still survive in Kirstenbosch Botanical Garden. It was essentially apartheid's first 'border'.

questions.question: The Taung Child, discovered in 1924, was identified by which anatomist?
questions.explanation: Raymond Dart identified the Taung Child in 1924 as Australopithecus africanus — a new species of early hominid. His claims were initially rejected by the scientific establishment but later vindicated.

questions.question: Which South African law required Black South Africans to carry a 'dompas' (reference book)?
questions.explanation: Ironically named the 'Abolition of Passes' Act, it actually replaced various pass documents with a single, more comprehensive reference book that ALL Black South Africans had to carry at all times. Classic apartheid doublespeak.

questions.question: How many people were killed in the Soweto Uprising of 16 June 1976, according to the official government count at the time?
questions.explanation: The apartheid government's official count was just 23, though the actual number is estimated at 176 to over 700. The government massively underreported to minimize the atrocity. The real toll may never be known.

questions.question: The Lydenburg Heads, among the earliest known African sculptures from south of the equator, date to approximately which century?
questions.explanation: The Lydenburg Heads date to around 500 AD (5th-6th century). Found in Mpumalanga, they are hollow ceramic heads and are among the earliest known examples of African sculpture south of the equator.
